page_title: When the House Heats Up — Comfort and Adaptation in Ethiopian Social Housing

sections.heading: When the house heats up, people open a window.
sections.subtitle: Ethiopia built hundreds of thousands of affordable flats — but few were designed for the heat inside them. A study of 317 homes in Jimma asks what 'comfortable' really means here, and how residents cope.

sections.heading: Comfort was an afterthought
sections.body: Climate change is pushing indoor temperatures up, and the discomfort lands hardest in low-income housing. Since 2005, Ethiopia's Integrated Housing Development Program has delivered vast numbers of low-cost units at speed — but thermal comfort was rarely part of how they were designed or built.
sections.figure_alt: Location map of Jimma city and the studied IHDP housing sites.
sections.insight: The same standardized blocks that solved a housing shortage left occupants to manage the heat on their own.

sections.heading: The gap
sections.quote: Limited attention has been given to thermal comfort in the design and construction of those affordable housing units.
sections.body: Comfort standards like ASHRAE 55 are built around modern, mechanically conditioned buildings. They travel poorly to naturally ventilated homes in the Global South, where occupants adapt so readily that a single 'neutral temperature' barely describes how they actually live.

sections.heading: Measuring comfort where people live
sections.body: Across the dry and wet seasons of 2023, the team monitored indoor environments with sensors and ran structured surveys in 317 IHDP houses in Jimma, drawn from a population of 1,520 households. They paired an adaptive comfort model with binary logistic regression to link how rooms feel to what residents actually do about it.
sections.figure_alt: Description of the survey respondents across the studied IHDP houses.

sections.heading: What the homes told us
sections.body: A local comfort temperature, and a behavior that switches on sharply once it's crossed.

stat_items.label: Adjusted local comfort temperature for these homes
Chance a window is open below the comfort threshold
Chance a window is open once it passes 30 °C
IHDP homes monitored across dry and wet seasons

sections.heading: A switch, not a dial
sections.body: Window opening doesn't ramp up gently with heat — it flips. Below the comfort temperature, residents mostly keep windows shut; above 30 °C, almost everyone opens them. Indoor and outdoor temperature are the strongest predictors of that choice, with the fitted model giving logit(Pw) = 0.448·Tin − 11.01 (n = 317, p < 0.001).
sections.figure_alt: Logistic relationship between the probability of window opening and temperature.
sections.insight: Window operability isn't a convenience here — it's the primary thermostat residents have.

sections.heading: How residents keep cool
sections.body: Opening windows is the front line, but it sits within a wider repertoire of low-tech adaptations — adjusting clothing, shifting activities, and using what few appliances are available. Where these options are blocked by building design or cost, occupants are left exposed, and that constraint is itself a design failure.
sections.figure_alt: Widely used adaptive measures occupants take to keep indoor space cool.

sections.heading: Design for the heat, not around it
sections.body: Comfort in these homes is built on behavior the design never planned for. Treating window operability, orientation, and ventilation as first-class concerns — not afterthoughts — would let Ethiopia's next wave of high-rise affordable housing keep residents comfortable instead of leaving them to improvise.
sections.cta: Integrate thermal comfort and window operability into the design of massive high-rise residential housing developments.

sections.heading: Credits
sections.authors: Belsti Asres, Jiayu Pan, Ronita Bardhan & Esayas Alemayehu
sections.affiliations: Dilla University · University of Cambridge · Jimma University. Published in Energy & Buildings 360 (2026) 117375.
